q: If a message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>m</mi></msub></math> is amplitude modulated with a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></math> and radiated through an antenna, the wavelength of the corresponding signal in air is
question: If a message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>m</mi></msub></math> is amplitude modulated with a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>f</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></math> and radiated through an antenna, the wavelength of the corresponding signal in air is
solution:
Given frequency of massage signal =fm frequency of carrier signal =fc
the wavelength of the corresponding signal in air is ⇒λ=cfc

Here, modulation index, μ=AmAc=2040=12

Modulated amplitude =μAc2

=12×402=10

[use radius of the earth=6400 km]
question:
A transmitting antenna at top of a tower has a height of 50 m, and the height of receiving antenna is, 80 m. What is the range of communication for the line of sight (LOS) mode?
[use radius of the earth=6400 km]
solution: The formula of range of the antenna is given by the formula,=2Rh1+2Rh2., here R is the radius of the earth then, putting the values, the range =57.28 km.

subject: Physics
q: The number of amplitude modulated broadcast stations that can be accommodated in a $300 \mathrm{kHz}$ band width for the highest modulating frequency $15 \mathrm{kHz}$ will be:
question: The number of amplitude modulated broadcast stations that can be accommodated in a $300 \mathrm{kHz}$ band width for the highest modulating frequency $15 \mathrm{kHz}$ will be:
solution:
Given, modulating frequency $f_m$ $=15 \mathrm{KHz}$
$\therefore \quad$ Bandwidth of one channel $=2 f_m=30 \mathrm{kHz}$
$\therefore \quad$ No of channels accommodate
$$
=\frac{300 \mathrm{kHz}}{30 \mathrm{kHz}}=10
$$

subject: Physics
q:
The power radiated from a linear antenna of length l is proportional to
(Given, λ= Wavelength of wave):
question:
The power radiated from a linear antenna of length l is proportional to
(Given, λ= Wavelength of wave):
solution:
Linear antennas are considered to be those antennas that imply the use of electrically thin conductors having diameter much less than the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave passing through them.

The formula to calculate the effective power radiated by a linear antenna is given by

Peff=kl2λ2

where, k is a constant.

subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>160</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts is made to vary between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>max</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>min</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts. The peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> of the modulating signal is,
question: A carrier wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>160</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts is made to vary between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>max</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>min</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> volts. The peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> of the modulating signal is,
solution:
Maximum amplitude, 
Amax=Am+AC
⇒Vmax=Vm+VC
200=Vm+160
Vm=40
∴ Peak voltage Am=40
Answer is 40.

subject: Physics
q: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: What should be the height of transmitting antenna and the population covered if the television telecast is to cover a radius of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>?</mo></math>  The average population density around the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Radius covered, r=2RHT
150 km=2×6.5×106 mHT
150 km×1032=2×6.5×106HT
HT=1731 m

Population covered =πr22000 km-2
=3.14×(150)2×2000=1413×105

subject: Physics
q: In an amplitude modulation, a modulating signal having amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is superimposed with a carrier signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> in first case. Then, in second case, the same modulating signal is superimposed with different carrier signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of modulation index in the two case respectively wil be :
question: In an amplitude modulation, a modulating signal having amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is superimposed with a carrier signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> in first case. Then, in second case, the same modulating signal is superimposed with different carrier signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The ratio of modulation index in the two case respectively wil be :
solution:
Modulating index is stated as μ=AmAc, where, Am=amplitude of modulating signal and Ac=amplitude of carrier signal.

Now, for first case, μ1=XY

For second case, μ2=X2Y

Thus, the ratio of μ1μ2=XYX2Y=21⇒μ1:μ2=2:1

subject: Physics
q:
The amplitude of 15sin(1000πt) is modulated by 10sin(4πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequency (ies) of

(A) 500 Hz

(B) 2 Hz

(C) 250 Hz

(D) 498 Hz

(E) 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The amplitude of 15sin(1000πt) is modulated by 10sin(4πt) signal. The amplitude modulated signal contains frequency (ies) of

(A) 500 Hz

(B) 2 Hz

(C) 250 Hz

(D) 498 Hz

(E) 502 Hz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The frequency components of amplitude modulated signal are fc-fm, fc, fc+fm

Here, carrier wave frequency is

fc=100π2π=500 Hz

Modulating wave frequency is 

fm=4π2π=2 Hz

So, the frequencies in amplitude modulated signal are =498 Hz, 500 Hz, 502 Hz

q: The physical sizes of the transmitter and receiver antenna in a communication system are:
question: The physical sizes of the transmitter and receiver antenna in a communication system are:
options: independent of both carrier and modulation frequency
solution: The physical sizes of transmitter and receiver antenna are directly proportional to carrier wave wavelength or inversely proportional to carrier wave frequency.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Match List-I with List-II

 

List-I

(Layer of atmosphere)

  

List-II

(Approximate height over earth's surface)
(A) F1-Layer (I) 10 km
B D-Layer II 170-190 km
C Troposphere III 100 km
D E-Layer V 65-75 km

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
The F1 layer is above the E layer and the ionization is less compared to E layer. It ranges to 170-190 km.

The D layer is located above the stratosphere. It ranges from 60-90 km above the earth's surface.

The troposphere starts from the ground and ranges to 100 km.

The E layer is located above the D layer. The intensity of ionization is less than the D layer. It ranges from 100-125 km above the earth's surface.

q: At a particular station, the TV transmission tower has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. To triple its coverage range, height of the tower should be increased to
question: At a particular station, the TV transmission tower has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. To triple its coverage range, height of the tower should be increased to
solution:
Let d be range of TV transmission.

From above figure, 

d2=h+R2-R2

 ⇒d2=h2+R2+2RH-R2

⇒d2=h2+2Rh

 As R≫≫h then, d≈2Rh     ...1

Now, if coverage is to be increased 3 times

3d=2Rh'    ...2

Dividing equation 2 by 1, we have

3d d=2Rh'2Rh

⇒9=h'h

9h=h'

If h=100 m, then tower of height 900 m is required.

q:
A modulating signal is a square wave, as shown in the figure.

If the carrier wave is given as ct=2sin8πt volts, the modulation index is:
question:
A modulating signal is a square wave, as shown in the figure.

If the carrier wave is given as ct=2sin8πt volts, the modulation index is:
solution:
Modulation index 'μ' is related to amplitude of modulation signal 'Am' and amplitude of carrier wave 'Ac' as:

μ=AmAc

Now, from graph Am=1 V and from equation of carrier wave Ac=2 V. Therefore,

μ=12.

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting antenna at the top of a tower has a height 32 m and the height of the receiving antenna is 50 m. What is the maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight (LOS) mode?
question: A transmitting antenna at the top of a tower has a height 32 m and the height of the receiving antenna is 50 m. What is the maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight (LOS) mode?
solution:
Given,

Height of transmitting antenna is, hT=50 m

Height of receiving antenna is, hR=32 m

Thus, maximum distance for satisfactory communication will be,

dM=2RhT+2RhR where R is radius of the Earth,

dM=2RhT+2RhRdM=2×64×105×50+2×64×105×32dM=800010+640010dM=1440010 mdM=45536.7 mdM=45.5 km

subject: Physics
q: A $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ transmitter emits radio waves of wavelength $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The number of photons emitted per second by the transmitter is of the order of
question: A $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ transmitter emits radio waves of wavelength $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The number of photons emitted per second by the transmitter is of the order of
solution:
Power $=\frac{n h c}{\lambda}$
(where, $n=$ no. of photons per second)
$$
\Rightarrow \quad n=\frac{10 \times 10^3 \times 500}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} \simeq 10^{31}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
We do not transmit low frequency signal to long distances because
(a) The size of the antenna should be comparable to signal wavelength which is unreal solution for a signal of longer wavelength.
(b) Effective power radiated by a long wavelength baseband signal would be high.
(c) We want to avoid mixing up signals transmitted by different transmitter simultaneously.
(d) Low frequency signal can be sent to long distances by superimposing with a high frequency wave as well.

Therefore, the most suitable option will be :
question:
We do not transmit low frequency signal to long distances because
(a) The size of the antenna should be comparable to signal wavelength which is unreal solution for a signal of longer wavelength.
(b) Effective power radiated by a long wavelength baseband signal would be high.
(c) We want to avoid mixing up signals transmitted by different transmitter simultaneously.
(d) Low frequency signal can be sent to long distances by superimposing with a high frequency wave as well.

Therefore, the most suitable option will be :
solution:
For longer wavelength, size of antenna would increase. The size of the antennas should be atleast λ4 for high efficiency of signal radiation. 

The effective power radiated by an antenna is inversely proportional to the square of the wavelength, so, for the same antenna length, the power radiated increases with decreasing wavelength or increasing frequency.Hence, the effective power radiated by a long-wavelength baseband signal would be small.

Also, mixing of signals needs to be avoided, as all the signals get mixed together and a receiver can not separate them from each other.  Also, we can use modulation to send low frequency signal by superimposing them with high frequency signals. Electrical audio signals are superimposed on a high frequency wave called the carrier wave. The carrier wave is generated from radio-frequency oscillators and is undamped in nature.

subject: Physics
q: The carrier frequency of a transmitter is provided by a tank circuit of a coil of inductance $49 \mu \mathrm{H}$ and a capactiance of $2.5 \mathrm{nF}$. It is modulated by an audio signal of $12 \mathrm{kHz}$. The frequency range occupied by the side bands is:
question: The carrier frequency of a transmitter is provided by a tank circuit of a coil of inductance $49 \mu \mathrm{H}$ and a capactiance of $2.5 \mathrm{nF}$. It is modulated by an audio signal of $12 \mathrm{kHz}$. The frequency range occupied by the side bands is:
solution:
Given: Inductance, $L=49 \mu \mathrm{H}=49 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{H}$, capacitance $C=2.5 \mathrm{nF}=2.5 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~F}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Using } \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}} \\
&=\frac{1}{\sqrt{49 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{2.5}{10} \times 10^{-9}}} \\
&=\frac{1}{7 \times 5 \times 10^{-8}}=\frac{10^8}{7 \times 5} \\
&\text { or, } \frac{10^8}{7 \times 5}=2 \pi \times f=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times f \\
&\text { or, } f=\frac{10^7}{22}=\frac{10^4}{22} \mathrm{kHz}=454.54 \mathrm{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore frequency range $454.54 \pm 12 \mathrm{kHz}$ i.e., $442 \mathrm{kHz}-466 \mathrm{kHz}$

q: A carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>512</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is amplitude modulated by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and transmitted through an antenna. What will be the bandwidth of the modulated signal ?
question: A carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>512</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is amplitude modulated by a message signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and transmitted through an antenna. What will be the bandwidth of the modulated signal ?
solution:
Band width =2fm

ωm=1.57×108=2πfm

BW=2fm=1082 Hz=50MHz

subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum amplitude of an amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index for this amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______. (Round off your answer to the nearest integer)
question: The maximum and minimum amplitude of an amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index for this amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______. (Round off your answer to the nearest integer)
solution:
Modulation index =Amax-AminAmax+Amin

=16-816+8=824=13=0.33

x×10-2=0.33

So, $x=33$

question: In amplitude modulation, the message signal Vm(t)=10sin2π×105t volts and carrier signal VCt=20sin2π×107t volts. The modulated signal now contains the message signal with lower side band and upper side band frequency, therefore the bandwidth of modulated signal is α kHz. The value of α is:
solution:
Bandwidth =2×fm

=2×105 Hz=200 kHz

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting antenna has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and that of receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight mode is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The value of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A transmitting antenna has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and that of receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight mode is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. The value of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Recall the formula of maximum distance between transmitting antenna and receiving antenna for satisfactory communication in terms of the radius of the earth and height of antennas, 

d=2Rh1+2Rh2⇒d=2×6400×0.32+2×6400×2≈224 km

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct statement for amplitude modulation
question: Choose the correct statement for amplitude modulation
options: Amplitude of modulating signal is varied in accordance with the information signal.
Amplitude of modulated signal is varied in accordance with the information signal.
Amplitude of carrier signal is varied in accordance with the information signal.
Amplitude of modulated signal is varied in accordance with the modulating signal.
solution: Amplitude modulation (AM) is a modulation technique used in electronic communication, most commonly for transmitting messages with a radio wave. In amplitude modulation, the amplitude (signal strength) of the carrier wave is varied in proportion to that of the information signal.

subject: Physics
q:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
question:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
solution:
Given: R=6.4×106 m

Using the relation between the range of the radio communication and the height of the transmission antenna hT and the receiving antenna hR, the maximum line of sight distance between two antennas, is dmax=2RhT+2RhR

⇒dmax=2×2×6.4×106×80=64000 m=64 km

subject: Physics
q: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: The height of transmitting antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the height of the receiving antenna is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>245</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The maximum distance between them for satisfactory communication in line of sight will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
The maximum distance for line of sight communication is 

d=2hR+2h'R

The given data is 

R=6400 kmh=180 m

h'=245 m

Thus, the value is

d=2×180×6400×103

+2×245×6400×103

=6×80×102+7×80×102

=104 km

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements

Statement I : For transmitting a signal, size of antenna (l) should be comparable to wavelength of signal (at least l =λ4 in dimension).

Statement II : In amplitude modulation, amplitude of carrier wave remains constant (unchanged).

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements

Statement I : For transmitting a signal, size of antenna (l) should be comparable to wavelength of signal (at least l =λ4 in dimension).

Statement II : In amplitude modulation, amplitude of carrier wave remains constant (unchanged).

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
solution: In amplitude modulation frequency of carrier wave remains unchanged. Minimum size of antenna should be 14th of wavelength so that the antenna can sense the variations of the signal properly.

subject: Physics
q: To radiate EM signal of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> with high efficiency, the antennas should have a minimum size equal to:
question: To radiate EM signal of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> with high efficiency, the antennas should have a minimum size equal to:
solution: For efficient radiation and reception, the height of transmitting and receiving antennas should be comparable to a quarter of wavelength of the frequency used. So for efficient transmission of signals of wavelengths λ the minimum length of antenna should be λ4.

question: The wavelength of the carrier waves in a modern optical fiber communication network is close to:
solution: For minimum attenuation, the wavelength of carrier waves is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>

q: For VHF signal broadcasting, _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>km</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> of maximum service area will be covered by an antenna tower of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> if the receiving antenna is placed at ground. Let radius of the earth be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Round off to the Nearest Integer) (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>)
question: For VHF signal broadcasting, _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>km</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> of maximum service area will be covered by an antenna tower of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> if the receiving antenna is placed at ground. Let radius of the earth be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Round off to the Nearest Integer) (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>)
solution:
d=2Rh

A=πd2

A=π2Rh

=3.14×2×6400×301000

A=1205.76 km2

A≃1206 km2

subject: Physics
q: A modulated signal <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> has the form <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>300</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>200</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>400</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The carrier frequency <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math> , the modulating frequency (message frequency) <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m , </mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math>and the modulation index <math><mi>μ</mi></math> are respectively given by :
question: A modulated signal <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> has the form <math><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>300</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>200</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>400</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></mrow></mrow></math> The carrier frequency <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math> , the modulating frequency (message frequency) <math><msub><mrow><mi>f</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m , </mi></mrow></msup></mrow></msub></math>and the modulation index <math><mi>μ</mi></math> are respectively given by :
solution:
Compare with

C m (t)= A c sin( w c t)+ μ A c 2 [ cos( w c − w m )t−cos( w c + w m ) ]

μ A c 2 =10

A c =30

μ= 2 3

w c =2π f c =300π ⇒ f c =150Hz

w c − w m =200π

w m =100π

f m =50Hz

subject: Physics
q:
A TV transmission tower antenna is at a height of 20 m. Suppose that the receiving antenna is at.
(i) ground level
(ii) a height of 5 m.
The increase in antenna range in case (ii) relative to case (i) is n%. The value of n, to the nearest integer, is.
question:
A TV transmission tower antenna is at a height of 20 m. Suppose that the receiving antenna is at.
(i) ground level
(ii) a height of 5 m.
The increase in antenna range in case (ii) relative to case (i) is n%. The value of n, to the nearest integer, is.
solution:
Range =2Rh

Range (i)=2Rh

Range (ii) =2Rh+2Rh'

where h=20 m & h'=5 m

Ans =2Rh'2Rh×100%=520×100%=50%

subject: Physics
q: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AM</mi></math> modulation, a signal is modulated on a carrier wave such that maximum and minimum amplitude are found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index is
question: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AM</mi></math> modulation, a signal is modulated on a carrier wave such that maximum and minimum amplitude are found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The modulation index is
solution:
Given here, Vmax=6 V and Vmin=2 V

Now, modulation index =Vmax-VminVmax+Vmin.×100%

=6-26+2×100%=50%

subject: Physics
q: If the highest frequency modulating a carrier is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>, then the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> broadcast stations accommodated in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> bandwidth are
question: If the highest frequency modulating a carrier is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>, then the number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> broadcast stations accommodated in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> bandwidth are
solution:
B. W. (Bandwidth) =2× maximum frequency at modulating signal

=2×5 kHz

=10 kHz

∴ No of stations accommodate =9010=9
